elements.sent-2411-001: This paper was supported by the CRISP center in JUMP 1.0 and the PRISM center in JUMP 2.0, Semiconductor Research Corporation (SRC) programs sponsored by DARPA.
elements.sent-2411-002: The rich set of tools available on server CPUS may not be available in virtualized environments or on the processors used in PIM or NMP systems, making it difficult to understand whether code fully uses the available memory performance.
elements.sent-2411-003: This work presents \projectName, a novel target-independent lightweight memory performance introspection framework.
elements.sent-2411-004: We make use of a basic block profiler to statically record instruction mix and insert instrumentation for regions of interest (ROI) in code, and use runtime timing and execution counts to calculate achieved bandwidth during post processing.
elements.sent-2411-005: We verify that \projectName's instrumentation is accurate and has low overhead on an x86-64 system, and demonstrate its portability by using it on 4 different ISAs and comparing the results reported for three servers.. \mnote{And any other.}
elements.sent-2411-006: We now describe each component of \projectName in more detail, as well as a set of optimizations implemented to improve profile accuracy and reduce instrumentation overhead.
elements.sent-2411-007: The IR pass is responsible for identifying code within regions of interest and statically analyzing the basic blocks that comprise them.
elements.sent-2411-008: LLVM provides a rich suite of analysis passes that can be used to organize the control flow graph (CFG) and identify useful properties such as domination and loop iteration counts.
elements.sent-2411-009: During compilation, clang translates inline assembly comments marking the beginning and end of a ROI.
elements.sent-2411-010: Additionally, since a ROI may only contain a subset of a basic block, the IR pass splits the basic blocks containing the ROI begin and end statements to prevent the instrumentation of unrelated instructions.
elements.sent-2411-011: Once the blocks belonging to the ROI are selected, \projectName constructs a basic block profiler with lightweight and thread local software counters.
elements.sent-2411-012: Each counter is associated with a set of basic blocks and records the number of times the first block of that set is executed.
elements.sent-2411-013: In order to guarantee that each block in the set is executed together, \projectName constructs the sets using a post-domination analysis.
elements.sent-2411-014: That is, by examining the CFG structure, \projectName groups basic blocks together such that if the first block in a set is executed, the subsequent blocks in that set are guaranteed to also be executed.
elements.sent-2411-015: We elaborate more on how these sets are constructed in Section \ref{sec:design-opt .}
elements.sent-2411-016: These post-domination sets are a variant of super blocks introduced by Agrawal~\cite{agrawal1994dominators .}
elements.sent-2411-017: However, Agrawal was targeting code coverage, while \projectName needs execution counts, requiring a mechanism to efficiently track the number of times each block is run.
elements.sent-2411-018: This is complicated by the potential presence of cycles in the CFG which can result in varying execution counts for blocks in the same set.
elements.sent-2411-019: We explain how \projectName resolves this challenge in Section \ref{sec:instrument_loop .}
elements.sent-2411-020: The IR pass inserts function calls at the beginning and end of each ROI.
elements.sent-2411-022: First, they add timing instructions to keep a running total of time spent in each ROI.
elements.sent-2411-023: Second, they include control logic to enable or disable counters for the region.
elements.sent-2411-024: This keeps track of the number of times an ROI is hit and enable counters every Nth time for a sampling period of N.
elements.sent-2411-025: Finally, the instrumentation reads hardware counters in addition to the time stamps when hardware counters are available and enabled in \projectName.
elements.sent-2411-026: We use the perf event library and read L1 misses, LLC misses, and total instructions executed by default.
elements.sent-2411-027: However, users can update the handler, which consists of less than 50 lines of C++ code, to leverage other methods of accessing counters or to record other events.
elements.sent-2411-028: We add counter reads at the start and end of each ROI to measure the total stats for the ROI.
elements.sent-2411-029: These reads are placed outside the timing events, before the starting timer call and after the ending timer call to ensure that the overhead associated with a perf event read does not pollute ROI timing measurements.
elements.sent-2411-030: The IR pass statically allocates the software counters as an array in thread-local memory.
elements.sent-2411-031: It also inserts inlinable function calls to update counters for each set of basic blocks.
elements.sent-2411-032: These functions accept arguments that are used to index into the counter array and increment the corresponding value.
elements.sent-2411-033: Additional care must be taken when multiple ROI are involved with shared functions to ensure that execution of a function from within one ROI does not impact the profiles of the other ROI.
elements.sent-2411-034: We describe this challenge and how \projectName resolves it in more detail in Section \ref{sec:functions .}
elements.sent-2411-035: Lastly, on application or thread termination, inserted code writes the software counter values and ROI statistics out to a file for later post processing.
elements.sent-2411-036: Write something about what events we can count - what is default, what can be done, what is hard (e.g., arguments as can't aggregate over loops)}\anote{Done \projectName counts instruction-level events within ROI.
elements.sent-2411-037: By default, it counts the number of instructions that access memory and the size of the argument, allowing it to compute the total bytes accessed.
elements.sent-2411-038: As instrumentation is done in an LLVM IR pass, which is architecture agnostic, local variable and parameter access is represented by variables that may be on the stack or in registers.
elements.sent-2411-039: This is because many stack accesses are due to register spilling or saving across function calls, which are not visible at the IR level.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, it cannot count events related to instruction parameters, such as addresses or values, as they aren't available statically and can change at runtime, which would require instrumenting and recording information about every execution of an instruction.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/ZRay_Post_DomSet.pdf} \caption{Example post-domination set. Block C post-dominates block A; one counter covers the two blocks. Block B does not post-dominate A, and is given its own counter.} \label{fig:postdomset}
elements.sent-2411-041: If the goal is to capture all memory instructions performed in the ROI of the application, a simple approach would be to maintain counters for these instruction types and increment them on every memory instruction execution.
elements.sent-2411-042: This can easily be done using Intel's Pintool~\cite{Pin by examining each executed instruction at the cost of significant instrumentation overhead and an architectural dependency on x86-64.}
elements.sent-2411-043: By recognizing the simple properties of basic blocks (single entry/exit points), a single software counter in combination with a collected data profile for the basic block can account for all instructions within the block as they will be executed together barring any exceptions or failures.
elements.sent-2411-044: By grouping basic blocks into post-domination sets, one software counter can now cover multiple basic blocks.
elements.sent-2411-045: When creating the post-domination sets, \projectName's IR pass will select the first basic block in the ROI and add it to a set with other basic blocks that post-dominate it.
elements.sent-2411-046: It repeats this process with the remaining basic blocks that did not post-dominate the first selected basic block until all basic blocks are assigned a set.
elements.sent-2411-047: With the sets constructed, the IR pass allocates a software counter for each post-domination set, and inserts counter increment instructions into the first basic block of each set.
elements.sent-2411-048: Then, the IR pass records the static instruction mix information of the basic blocks comprising each set and writes this information to the static mix file for use by the post-processing tool.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/ZRay_Figure_loop.pdf} \caption{Example loop SW counter hoisting. One counter covers both for loops by scaling the instruction mixes of the basic blocks by the loop trip counts.} \label{fig:loop_hoist}
elements.sent-2411-049: For each loop, \projectName uses LLVM's analysis framework to identify the loop's trip count (number of loop iterations to be performed).
elements.sent-2411-050: If the trip count can be identified at compile time, the static instruction mix of the post-domination set owned by the loop's header block are multiplied by the trip count when the IR pass writes them to the static mix file.
elements.sent-2411-051: The static mix is now representative of the instructions executed per entire loop execution.
elements.sent-2411-052: For improved performance on loops with identified trip counts, \projectName hoists the respective software counter above the header block to a new basic block so that it is only executed once per entire loop execution.
elements.sent-2411-053: Blocks that do not post-dominate the header (e.g., conditional statements within the loop) are treated as not belonging to a cycle and are instrumented normally.
elements.sent-2411-054: If the trip count can only be determined at runtime (e.g., controlled by a variable), \projectName's instrumentation uses the dynamic trip count to update the loop header's count in one operation, rather than incrementing the count once each executed iteration.
elements.sent-2411-055: If LLVM is unable to calculate the loop trip count at either compile or runtime (e.g., the trip count is not loop invariant), \projectName falls back to instrumenting all of the loop's blocks without any scaling applied, requiring a counter increment for each loop iteration.
elements.sent-2411-056: A complication in this scheme arises when nested loops are involved as shown in Figure \ref{fig:loop_hoist , where we have a parent and child for loop.}
elements.sent-2411-057: While one option would be to simply parse each loop individually, if both loops' trip counts can be calculated at compile time, we can combine the trip counts from each loop by applying the parent's trip count to its unique blocks and the product of the parent and child's trip counts to the child's basic blocks.
elements.sent-2411-058: With this adjustment, a single software counter can cover basic blocks across both loops and reduce instrumentation overhead substantially.
elements.sent-2411-059: We can continue this process and hoist software counters further up the CFG so long as each loop's trip count can be calculated at compile time and the parent loop's header is post-dominated by the subsequent child loop's header.
elements.sent-2411-060: ROI may spend most of their runtime in called functions, executing instructions not explicitly within the ROI code.
elements.sent-2411-061: Ignoring function calls sacrifices too much accuracy in most programs, and instrumenting all called functions will cause overhead on code paths outside the ROI as well as pollute the instrumentation logs.
elements.sent-2411-062: To address these issues, \projectName's IR pass uses {\em function cloning to create copies of functions called within ROI and applies instrumentation to these clones.}
elements.sent-2411-063: For example, if ROI $A$ calls function $B$, \projectName will create a function clone $B^*$ with instrumentation and replace all function calls to $B$ inside $A$ with $B^*$.
elements.sent-2411-064: This cloning is only done once and additional ROI calling $B$ will be directed to $B^*$ as well.
elements.sent-2411-065: However, the challenge still remains in distinguishing counter increments when multiple ROI call the same cloned function.
elements.sent-2411-066: As an example, suppose ROI $A$ and ROI $C$ both use $B^*$, an instrumented clone that requires 5 counters to record accesses to its basic blocks.
elements.sent-2411-067: How can \projectName ensure that $A$ calling $B^*$ will only update its own information and not impact the collected profile of $C$? \projectName resolves this challenge by allocating a separate set of counters corresponding to a cloned function for each ROI during the IR pass -- in this example it allocate 5 counters each to $A$ and $C$ for recording accesses in $B^*$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Function cloning only works if \projectName has access to the function definition during the IR pass.
elements.sent-2411-069: At the moment, there are two cases that \projectName cannot automatically handle: indirect functions and external library functions.
elements.sent-2411-070: For the first case, the developer can manually determine which functions are likely to be called and instrument them, analogous to manually performing function cloning.
elements.sent-2411-071: For example, if an indirect function call in an instrumented function $A$ can only call functions $B$ or $C$, the developer can also add ROIs to $B$ and $C$ to ensure that all accesses of interest will be recorded.
elements.sent-2411-072: For the second, a developer could compile the library code down to IR and merge it with the application's IR with llvm-link before applying \projectName's pass to it.
elements.sent-2411-073: Any functions called from the linked library will now be instrumented via function cloning.
elements.sent-2411-074: LLVM uses intrinsics for some standard library functions, particularly the memory movement/setting functions memcpy, memmove, and memset are critical for accurate measurements.
elements.sent-2411-075: The IR pass records the size of the intrinsic operation, if known at compile time, to the static mix file.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, the operation size may be unknown at compile time.
elements.sent-2411-077: In such cases, the IR pass can identify which variable holds the operation size.
elements.sent-2411-078: Since we can only determine the size of these operations at runtime, \projectName adds instructions at compile time to use this variable at runtime to record the number of bytes accessed, similar to counter array instrumentation for basic block counts.
elements.sent-2411-079: To avoid aggregating intrinsic access sizes with basic block counts, \projectName allocates separate data structures to record these unknown intrinsic sizes, the load and store intrinsic size array.
elements.sent-2411-080: Memmove and memcpy involve reads and writes increment operations to both load and store arrays; memset only updates the store intrinsic array.
elements.sent-2411-081: This approach allows us to cheaply and accurately record the number of {\em bytes read from or written to memory for these instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-082: However, under the hood, the standard library may perform transfers at any granularity of accesses, with potentially varying sizes, e.g., 4 or 8 bytes.
elements.sent-2411-083: This approach underestimates the count of {\em memory accesses when transfers are performed at smaller granularity.}
elements.sent-2411-084: The post-processing tool combines static information (instruction mix, bytes accessed) collected by the IR pass with dynamic information (software counters, timing information) collected by the inserted instrumentation at runtime.
elements.sent-2411-085: The final logged results comprise: counter values, dynamic instruction mix, region timing information, and achieved memory bandwidth estimates.
elements.sent-2411-086: The post-processing tool calculates dynamic instruction mix by multiplying the static instruction mix for each post-domination set with its respective software counter value.
elements.sent-2411-087: The post-processing tool also adds the number of bytes accessed and estimated count of memory access operations for intrinsics from the intrinsic size array to the dynamic mix at this point.
elements.sent-2411-088: The number of bytes read and written by the ROI is divided by ROI timing information to estimate achieved read and write memory bandwidths respectively.
elements.sent-2411-089: However, following this naive approach leads to \projectName reporting lower memory bandwidth than an ideal profiler would due to counter instrumentation overhead, which slows ROI execution.
elements.sent-2411-090: We account for instrumentation overhead using a heuristic {\em correction factor ($CF$) derived from the increase in instruction count due to counters.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Each counter increment involves a read-modify-write.
elements.sent-2411-092: Memory operations are generally among the most expensive, with even L1 accesses being several times more expensive than ALU operations.
elements.sent-2411-093: To account for this, we scale the counter instruction count in our correction factor by a memory factor, derived from the original memory access fraction (OMAF) of the ROI before counters were inserted and the new memory access fraction (NMAF) after inserting counters.
elements.sent-2411-094: Optimizing this heuristic is an option for further improvement on \projectName. \begin{equation}\text{OMAF}=\frac{\text{Total Memory Accesses}}{\text{Instructions Exec.}} \end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{NMAF}=\frac{\text{No. Counter Incr.}*2 + \text{Total Memory Accesses}}{\text{No. Counter Incr.}*2 + \text{Instructions Exec.}} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{Memory Factor (MF)}=\frac{\text{NMAF}}{\text{OMAF}} \\\end{equation} \begin{equation}CF=\frac{\text{Instructions Exec.} + (\text{No. Counter Incr.} * \text{MF})}{\text{Instructions Exec.}}\end{equation} Finally memory access bandwidths are reported as follows: \begin{equation}\text{ROI Read/Write BW} = CF * \frac{\text{ROI Bytes Read/Written}}{\text{ROI Total Time}}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}NMAF=\frac{No. Counter Incr.*2 + Total Memory Accesses}{No. Counter Incr.*2 + Instructions Exec.}\end{equation}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}ROI Read/Write BW = CF * \frac{ROI Bytes Read/Written}{ROI Total Time}\end{equation}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\linewidth]{images/cc-sv-time-series-bandwidth.pdf} \caption{Time series plot of read bandwidth for the Shiloach-Vishkin connectivity algorithm as reported by \projectName (upper plot) and DRAM bandwidth (lower plot) calculated using the number of LLC misses. X-axis is the snapshot number, analogous to time. It is the polling period to which the corresponding bandwidth measurement belongs to. } \label{fig:bw-time-series}
elements.sent-2411-095: Waiting until program termination to check memory performance is impractical for long running or continuous applications.
elements.sent-2411-096: To address these scenarios, we built a message-based monitor on top of \projectName.
elements.sent-2411-097: The monitoring process periodically polls counter values and executes the post-processing logic to calculate performance statistics for each epoch.
elements.sent-2411-098: Plotting these statistics over time can reveal phases in program execution, as well as thread imbalance, with an example shown in Figure \ref{fig:bw-time-series .}
elements.sent-2411-099: In this example, we study the bandwidth for an implementation of the Shiloach-Vishkin connected components calculation algorithm in the GAPBS benchmark suite, a data-intensive workload with a high LLC MPKI.
elements.sent-2411-100: We configure OpenMP to spawn 4 threads for simplifying the visualization.
elements.sent-2411-101: We record read bandwidth as captured by \projectName.
elements.sent-2411-102: Additionally, we enable \projectName's optional hardware performance monitoring and record LLC miss information to calculate DRAM bandwidth usage.
elements.sent-2411-103: The real DRAM bandwidth numbers in this case, surprisingly, are somewhat higher than those reported by \projectName.
elements.sent-2411-104: One reason for this could be that the LLC reads from DRAM at the granularity of 64 bytes.
elements.sent-2411-105: On the other hand, \projectName measures the accuracy of heap accesses to L1 keeping in mind the exact size of data type and data requested, which is usually 4 or 8 bytes.
elements.sent-2411-106: The workload involves extensive pointer chasing over neighbors of each node.
elements.sent-2411-107: Thus, it is likely that each cache line is accessed only one or a few times.
elements.sent-2411-108: In essence, we are fetching 64 bytes from DRAM for \textless 64B of read operations.
elements.sent-2411-109: We tested \projectName with other workloads in the GAPBS \cite{beamer2017gap suite and found that it similarly captured information about the bandwidth behavior for other workloads with high LLC MPKIs.}
elements.sent-2411-110: For more cache-friendly workloads with lower MPKI, \projectName is no longer able to capture the behavior, but it does identify trends such as sharp rises or drops in bandwidth usage.
elements.sent-2411-111: Any interface that allows for memory access can support this form of monitoring, such as remote monitoring over RDMA, as the monitor is simply copying memory.
elements.sent-2411-112: In practice, we found monitoring to involve copying on the order of tens of KB every couple seconds for large instrumented programs (1000+ counters).
elements.sent-2411-113: Users may adjust the intensity of instrumentation and the polling rate to reduce communication demands or capture more information.
elements.sent-2411-114: We report the number of lines of C++ code needed to implement each module of \projectName in Table \ref{loc-table .}

elements.sent-2411-115: We answer the following questions in this evaluation:.
elements.sent-2411-116: What is \projectName's accuracy for reported bytes read and written by heap accesses?.
elements.sent-2411-117: What is the accuracy of heap access bandwidth reported by \projectName?.
elements.sent-2411-118: What is the overhead of \projectName, and how effective is sampling in reducing overhead?.
elements.sent-2411-119: What is the effort to port \projectName to another architecture?.
elements.sent-2411-120: We determine \projectName's heap access accuracy to evaluate its ability to capture information for operations which are likely to result in DRAM accesses, because stack accesses have high cache hit rates.
elements.sent-2411-121: Low overhead ensures developers receive timely results, and allow it to be used for continuous profiling.
elements.sent-2411-122: We developed \projectName to leverage compute available on new memory devices.
elements.sent-2411-123: For this reason, it needs to be portable and have few (if any) dependencies on the target architecture.
elements.sent-2411-124: We examine \projectName's portability with a study examining the process of porting \projectName to an AArch64 server.
elements.sent-2411-125: We use a custom version of LLVM-15, with custom pragmas to demarcate the beginning and end of regions of interest.\mnote{Fix preceding sentence - it is unclear what we mean about pragmas}\anote{We did most of our testing with our version of clang, which had custom pragmas to mark the beginning and ending of ROI (and for a long time, we also had the MIR pass) and that is the one we have best accuracy with.}
elements.sent-2411-126: With the package manager's version of clang, accuracy is mostly unchanged but is worse for a small number of workloads, probably a new optimization that needs to be accounted for We tested \projectName with apt's version of llvm-15 and clang-15 for the portability experiments using asm comments instead of pragmas.
elements.sent-2411-127: We measured accuracy using a pintool that records stats for developer annotated regions of code as baseline on the x86-64 system.
elements.sent-2411-128: We ran each workload thrice and recorded the arithmetic mean of runtimes for the overhead experiments.
elements.sent-2411-129: We used Pin 3.31 to run the pintool.
elements.sent-2411-130: The pintool uses function instrumentation to identify developer annotated ROI.
elements.sent-2411-131: Within these ROI we track heap memory accesses.
elements.sent-2411-132: We use pin's ability to track the base registers for memory operands to filter out accesses using RSP.
elements.sent-2411-133: While DynamoRIO \cite{DynamoRIO-bruening2001design is compatible with AArch64 \cite{aarch64-dbi , it lacks some support for some of the functionality needed by our pintool for function-level instrumentation.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: We compare the bandwidth reported for a region of interest using \projectName against the baseline bandwidth which is calculated using the bytes accessed in the region of interest as reported by a pintool and recording time spent in the region of interest without instrumentation.
elements.sent-2411-135: We use an sm110p CloudLab\cite{CloudLab-Duplyakin+:ATC19 server with an Intel Xeon Silver 4314 CPU and 128 GB RAM for our accuracy and performance experiments.}
elements.sent-2411-137: We evaluated \projectName's portability using an AArch64 m400 CloudLab server with 64GB RAM running Ubuntu 22.04.
elements.sent-2411-138: We compare its stats against a Xeon Silver 4114 (in addition to the Xeon Silver 4314 server) due to their similar L1 cache capacities per-core.
elements.sent-2411-139: We test \projectName on general-purpose systems because real in- and near-memory systems are unavailable and simulations are slow, precluding us from evaluating \projectName with anything but the smallest of input sizes.
elements.sent-2411-140: We evaluate \projectName on x86-64 using all benchmarks in the GAPBS workload and all 16 benchmarks in the SPEC2017 suite written exclusively in C or C++ as per their documentation \cite{SPEC-benchmarks .}
elements.sent-2411-141: The GAPBS suite is representative of data-intensive workloads, while the SPEC suite helps us confirm that \projectName is effective at capturing information for a variety of workloads.
elements.sent-2411-142: We used the twitter datasets \cite{twitter-graph-dataset linked in the GAPBS repository as input to the GAPBS workloads, and used train workload sizes for SPEC.}
elements.sent-2411-143: We compiled the workloads with -O2 optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-144: The GAPBS workloads were built with OpenMP directives enabled and run with 32 threads, with SMT enabled.
elements.sent-2411-145: The SPEC workloads were run with SMT disabled.
elements.sent-2411-146: We only used the GAPBS workloads for evaluating portability on the AArch64 system, and were run with 8 threads on the x86-64 server chosen for comparison.
elements.sent-2411-147: We tried to follow the heuristic described in CoNDA \cite{CoNDA using function LLC MPKI and execution time for identifying data-intensive kernels where possible.}
elements.sent-2411-148: We were able to identify functions meeting the criteria in most of the GAPBS workloads.
elements.sent-2411-149: The methodology did not apply to the SPEC benchmark programs; for these, we determined the top 5 functions by execution time and selected frequently called kernels where possible.

A value of 1 indicates 100\% accuracy. Values greater and less than 1 represent over- and underestimation of byte counts, respectively. Vertical dotted red lines demarcate different benchmark suites. The last two bars represent geomean across both benchmark suites. } \label{fig:ii-byte-wo-stack}

elements.sent-2411-156: However, a branch within RE\_rayobject\_intersect leads to an indirect call to a RayObject's raycast function, which eventually leads to RE\_rayobject\_instance\_intersect being called.
elements.sent-2411-157: Our studies indicate that the vast majority of heap accesses within the ROI are reads.
elements.sent-2411-158: Missing a single indirect function that is more likely to write to memory than the rest of the code executed can lead to us missing a large fraction of writes, even if we capture most accesses.
elements.sent-2411-159: The SPEC nab workload shows poor load accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-160: We instrument the function mme34 in this workload.
elements.sent-2411-161: This inaccuracy results from standard library calls for mathematical operations.

elements.sent-2411-163: Overall, \projectName shows very high accuracy for measuring the count of heap bytes accessed, despite instrumenting the architecture-agnostic IR.

The last three bars represent geomean across both benchmark suites. } \label{fig:iii-x86-overhead}
elements.sent-2411-178: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:iii-x86-overhead , \projectName generally has low overhead, with instrumented binaries terminating in a time similar to that of uninstrumented versions.}
elements.sent-2411-179: For a few workloads, we observe an overhead of 2x or more when instrumenting every ROI access, as shown in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-180: Running mcf and povray binaries with perf attached indicated that they were core bound after instrumentation, with mcf initially being bottlenecked by bad speculation and povray initially being frontend bound.
elements.sent-2411-181: The workloads becoming core bound indicated that we likely saw an increase in dependencies between instructions.
elements.sent-2411-182: Counter increment operations always execute sequentially, with each instruction dependent on the prior instruction; we load an address, load a value from that address, increment it, and store it back.
elements.sent-2411-183: The total number of instructions executed also greatly increases in both cases, as measured by perf.
elements.sent-2411-184: These stats indicate that the workloads have a lot of instrumentation on the commonly taken paths.
elements.sent-2411-185: On examining the ROI, we found that the instrumented functions had many if-else control flow statements.
elements.sent-2411-186: Control-flow heavy functions tend to have small basic blocks, in terms of instruction count, as basic blocks always end at control flow instructions.
elements.sent-2411-187: Additionally, such ROI tend to be short-lived and frequently executed, which greatly increases the impact of timing events on execution time.
elements.sent-2411-188: Furthermore, \projectName's sampling checks further complicate control flow, adding control dependencies and hurting branch predictor performance for such workloads.
elements.sent-2411-189: This combination of factors -- adding many counters to small basic blocks for short-lived ROI is a pathological case for \projectName that can result from code with a lot of control flow.
elements.sent-2411-190: We note that control-flow-heavy workloads like these are unlikely to perform well on or be offloaded to accelerators.
elements.sent-2411-191: Developers using \projectName for memory bound systems are unlikely to ever encounter such cases.
elements.sent-2411-192: On the flip side, we see lbm, which exhibits very low runtime overhead.
elements.sent-2411-193: The ROI selected for lbm consisted of a large work loop with very little control flow.
elements.sent-2411-194: The amount of instrumentation added is negligible, so the instrumented binary essentially takes as long as an uninstrumented binary.
elements.sent-2411-195: The rest of the workloads fall somewhere between these two extremes.

elements.sent-2411-196: We incorporate optimizations like loop hoisting and post-domination sets to reduce the number of counters allocated and incremented by \projectName.

elements.sent-2411-198: Most workloads see small increases in overhead, in the range of 5-10\%, but parest and imagick are notable outliers.
elements.sent-2411-199: The ROI instrumented in both cases predominantly consist of loops.
elements.sent-2411-200: The number of counters allocated and increment operations inserted in code increase by almost 30\% for both workloads.
elements.sent-2411-201: Our sampling mechanism determines whether all counters must be incremented and whether time stamps be recorded at the start of each region of interest.
elements.sent-2411-202: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:iii-x86-overhead , most workloads see steep drops in overhead on sampling 1\% of ROI executions, with mcf and povray dropping from taking \textgreater 3x longer (when recording information for each and every ROI execution) as an uninstrumented binary to taking only 1.2x longer.}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/vi-perf-overhead.pdf} \caption{Runtime overhead observed with binaries instrumented using \projectName and perf compared to uninstrumented binaries. The last two bars represent geomean across both benchmark suites. } \label{fig:vi-x86-perf-overhead}
elements.sent-2411-203: Additionally, we examine the overhead for both benchmark suites when instrumented with \projectName with performance counters enabled.
elements.sent-2411-204: We record three hardware performance counter events using the perf\_event library, reading the counters six times per ROI call.
elements.sent-2411-205: As can be seen in Figure \ref{fig:vi-x86-perf-overhead recording these events adds considerable overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-206: Workloads like mcf and povray, which saw a slow down over 3x with \projectName are now over 80x slower.
elements.sent-2411-207: As previously discussed, the instrumented ROI in these workloads are called very frequently and involve executing very few instructions.
elements.sent-2411-208: While \projectName's core functionality added a small amount of overhead with its counters and timing events, 6 hardware counter reads are very expensive operations that end up completely dominate the execution.
elements.sent-2411-209: In our studies, we found that on average, a read to a hardware performance counter via perf on a modern x86 machine could take upwards of 150 ns.
elements.sent-2411-210: Given this high overhead of accessing hardware counters, they are best used with sampling.
elements.sent-2411-211: Platform-specific handlers that directly access performance counters (e.g., rdmsr/wrmsr on x86-64) could be used to improve performance further.. \if 0.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{images/iv-sampling-efficacy.pdf}\ \caption{Percentage of heap bytes accessed when instrumenting everything for a sampling rate of 1 in 100 and 1 in 10 ROI. We would ideally expect 1\% and 10\% of accesses respectively to be captured for the sampling to be representative. Y-axis follows the logarithmic scale. } \label{fig:iii-x86-overhead-pin}
elements.sent-2411-212: Sampling is usually effective, resulting in \projectName capturing a fraction of bytes proportional to the sampling rate, and does not affect bandwidth accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-213: We saw three notable cases where byte counts were off - bc, mcf and imagick.
elements.sent-2411-214: In all cases, this is because of the ROI selected.
elements.sent-2411-215: For instance, our instrumentation covers the main work loop which is only executed once in bc.
elements.sent-2411-216: As per our current policy, an ROI always has instrumentation enable the first time it executes.
elements.sent-2411-217: Thus, for such ROI, which are executed very few times, having instrumentation enabled just that one time is enough to capture the bulk of the information, rendering the sampling policy ineffective.
elements.sent-2411-218: We conclude our study on sampling efficacy with the observation that the current sampling policy is fairly effective, but needs to be made more robust to handle cases when ROI are executed very few times.

elements.sent-2411-235: Data movement is a major system bottleneck~\cite{Google-Workloads-Consumer , dominating runtime overheads and energy consumption.}
elements.sent-2411-236: A variety of approaches have been proposed and used to soften the memory wall.
elements.sent-2411-237: Some recent work \cite{Demystifying-CXL has proposed leveraging CXL to expand memory bandwidth in addition to capacity.}
elements.sent-2411-238: Additionally, there exists a rich body of research proposing processing-in-memory (PIM) and near-memory processing (NMP) systems to move computation closer to memory.
elements.sent-2411-239: These systems often make use of 3D stacked DRAM technology like High Bandwidth Memory (HBM) and Hybrid Memory Cube (HMC) with accelerators~\cite{CoNDA,ABNDP,SDAM,TETRIS .}
elements.sent-2411-240: Such architectures are unlikely to offer performance benefits for entire applications -- rather, developers will need to identify the most data-intensive sections of code in their applications~\cite{CoNDA, PIM-workload-driven-perspective that achieve significant performance or efficiency gains after accounting for offload overhead incurred.}
elements.sent-2411-241: Even CXL-based systems, which do not involve offloading code, require someone (usually the OS) to make decisions about data placement across memory tiers.
elements.sent-2411-242: Data-intensive code must take advantage of higher memory bandwidth by generating a large number of parallel memory accesses regardless of how bandwidth is made available.
elements.sent-2411-243: These highly parallel applications frequently make use of vectorization and multithreading~\cite{To-PIM-or-not , simultaneously issuing many accesses to different memory channels.}
elements.sent-2411-244: However, writing code which effectively uses such techniques is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-245: For instance, a developer may write or reuse code that leads to unequal distribution of work across threads, or use data\ structures with synchronization bottlenecks.

elements.sent-2411-247: The code snippet involves pointer chasing for each node to transform each tree to which a node belongs into one of unit height (lines 3 and 4.) With five OpenMP threads for the example graph, OpenMP's static scheduling policy assigns thread 1 to iteration 1, thread 2 to iteration 2 etc, with each thread assigned to a core with private L1 cache.
elements.sent-2411-248: Per line 3, threads 1, 2 and 3 will make 1 memory access (since their parent and grandparent nodes are the same), while, threads 4 and 5 will need to make 2 accesses, leading to unequal bandwidth usage across threads.
elements.sent-2411-249: Workload imbalance across cores is but one factor that may prevent an application from optimally using bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-250: Some other factors that could lead to the same include inefficient data structure layouts, and poor memory placement in NUMA systems.

elements.sent-2411-257: Identifying such issues require tools that can analyze the most data-intensive subsections of applications.

elements.sent-2411-262: We introduce \projectName, a low-overhead framework for memory performance introspection at the granularity of developer annotated subsets of programs, which we term {\em regions of interest (ROI).}
elements.sent-2411-263: We have three high-level goals for \projectName: first, we seek a system capable of instrumenting arbitrary parts of a program with low runtime overhead to be suitable for continuous profiling or instrumenting offloadable code.
elements.sent-2411-264: Second, we seek portability to a wide range of ISAs and accelerators by minimizing dependence on archictecture-specific features.
elements.sent-2411-265: Finally, we want to instrument multi-threaded workloads and generate per-thread measurements.
elements.sent-2411-266: If performance counters are available, it can snapshot them before and after a ROI and store the difference.
elements.sent-2411-267: It adds counters that are incremented at runtime and are used in combination with the information generated during compilation to measure the memory bandwidth and instructions executed for a region of interest.
elements.sent-2411-268: Furthermore, we implement optimizations to reduce the amount of instrumentation to less than one per basic block.
elements.sent-2411-269: We implement \projectName on LLVM, allowing it to be ported to a wide variety of devices, with the majority of its code being implemented in an IR pass, which is architecture agnostic.
elements.sent-2411-270: It supports multi-threaded execution with per-thread counters, and uses sampling to reduce overhead.
elements.sent-2411-271: While we focus on memory bandwidth, \projectName can also record other information about ROI execution.
elements.sent-2411-272: We evaluate \projectName on 24 applications from the GAPBS~\cite{beamer2017gap and SPEC2017~\cite{SPEC2017 benchmark suites.}}
elements.sent-2411-273: In comparison, pintool runtime is 5-7x longer.
elements.sent-2411-274: We compare counts and data volumes of loads and stores for these benchmark suites against a pintool, and find \projectName's accuracy is about 93\% for data volume (bytes accessed) and 87\% for bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-275: We demonstrate \projectName's portability with an evaluation on an AArch64 system, and additionally confirm its functionality on RISC-V and POWER8 systems.
elements.sent-2411-276: We introduce \projectName, a novel memory performance introspection framework providing detailed performance information about user-annotated regions of interest.
elements.sent-2411-277: We describe an optimized instrumentation process that can accurately obtain information about the instruction mix and memory bandwidth for regions of interest with low overhead.
elements.sent-2411-278: We designed \projectName as a target-independent memory performance introspection tool to support a variety of use cases and hardware environments.
elements.sent-2411-279: Specifically, we designed \projectName to accurately report information about memory operations and their performance both at the granularity of a region being instrumented as well as for its component basic blocks for more fine grained analysis and tuning of program behavior.
elements.sent-2411-280: With the rise of domain specific computing and the plethora of architectures being proposed to tackle the memory wall, we seek an approach that minimizes the effort to port it to a new architecture.
elements.sent-2411-281: With parallel processing needed to fully saturate memory bandwidth, instrumentation must be thread safe.
elements.sent-2411-282: Finally, instrumentation must be lightweight to minimize the impact on achieved memory bandwidth if accurate bandwidth estimates are to be made.
elements.sent-2411-283: Figure \ref{fig:zray_general_overview illustrates the general relationship between these components as an application is profiled.}
elements.sent-2411-284: To instrument an application with \projectName, users mark the regions of interest (ROI) they wish to profile using pragma statements to specify the begin and end boundaries.
elements.sent-2411-285: A developer may use one of many techniques described in literature to identify data-intensive code.
elements.sent-2411-286: This could either involve profiling applications and identifying how frequently they access memory, using stats like L1 and LLC miss rates~\cite{DAMOV , or device-specific heuristics~\cite{To-PIM-or-not which take into account the cost of offload and the potential speedup from offloading the ROI code to an accelerator.}}
elements.sent-2411-287: In addition to profiling ROIs, \projectName supports instrumenting all basic blocks in the program, unlike standard profiling tools like gprof that instrument functions only.
elements.sent-2411-288: This can be used to help identify the most frequently accessed basic blocks and functions, then mark ROIs accordingly as well as those that are most memory intensive and likely to benefit from offload.
elements.sent-2411-289: After annotating ROI, users compile their application to an IR file format that \projectName can operate on.
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[width=0.75\linewidth]{images/ZRay_Figures_C_to_C.pdf} \caption{\projectName applies timing events and software counters to regions of interest.} \label{fig:zray_c_to_c}
elements.sent-2411-290: The pass inserts code to statically allocate software counters at program start, with counter increment and timing instructions inserted into the ROI at the identified insertion points as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:zray_c_to_c .}
elements.sent-2411-291: In environments where hardware performance counters are available, \projectName enables users to snapshot hardware performance counters before and after an ROI along with timing data.
elements.sent-2411-292: The post-processing routine runs after application termination and produces output files containing the dynamic instruction mix, timing information, achieved memory bandwidth, and performance counter stats (when available) per thread for every ROI.
elements.sent-2411-293: Additionally, the post-processing routine also logs counter, instruction and memory statistics per thread for every basic block instrumented to a CSV file for user analysis with software like pandas or R.
elements.sent-2411-294: The post-processing routine calculates these stats by combining software counter values with the instruction mix information recorded at compile time.

elements.sent-2411-297: Overall, we see three use cases for \projectName:.
elements.sent-2411-298: For applications bottlenecked by memory, identify and profile regions of code responsible for such bottlenecks and make algorithmic optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-299: While developing code for NMP or PIM devices to identify offload targets as discussed previously.
elements.sent-2411-300: On NMP or PIM devices, once they are made available, to measure achieved performance.
elements.sent-2411-301: Pin~\cite{Pin allows the user to design a custom tool to collect whatever specific information they require.}
elements.sent-2411-302: However, Pin only supports a few ISAs and incurs relatively high overhead.
elements.sent-2411-303: Valgrind~\cite{Valgrind provides features for memory debugging and profiling, but also has high overhead.}
elements.sent-2411-304: DynInst~\cite{DynInst can be used to dynamically instrument binaries without recompilation or re-linking.}
elements.sent-2411-305: However, it requires substantial porting for new architectures and had overheads closer to Pin.
elements.sent-2411-306: LLVM-based tools maintain portability by operating at the IR level, but preexisting tools still do not meet our use-case requirements.
elements.sent-2411-307: PISA~\cite{PISA and its follow-up work PISA-NMC~\cite{PISA-NMC use the LLVM infrastructure for hardware-agnostic instrumentation of workloads.}}
elements.sent-2411-308: PISA provides information about instruction level parallelism, memory access patterns and branch entropy.
elements.sent-2411-309: PISA-NMC extracts information about memory entropy, data reuse distance, data-level parallelism and basic-block level parallelism for near-memory computing architectures.
elements.sent-2411-310: However, such instrumentation on average resulted in an increase in execution time by two to three orders of magnitude, with the designers intending users to perform analysis only once per application and dataset.
elements.sent-2411-311: DINAMITE~\cite{miucin2016dinamite is an LLVM-based memory profiling tool which logs memory accesses, models cache behavior, and can be used to identify potential memory bottlenecks due to poor data layout or multi-threaded contention.}
elements.sent-2411-312: However, DINAMITE's instrumentation incurs overhead similar to PIN, which interferes with our use case in estimating achieved memory bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-313: LLVM Machine Code Analyzer (llvm-mca) uses processor models to simulate and calculate ROI performance and resource pressure at the assembly level.
elements.sent-2411-314: However, llvm-mca ROI simulations do not account for program logic outside the ROI, or thread contention, that may influence actual memory performance.
